[ a Samaritane . ] Samaria be the city royal of the ten tribe , build by Omri and remain the chief seat of the kingdom so long as their kingdom endure : 1 king . 16.24 . but when Salamansur the assyrian ( in the day of Hoshea ) besiege it , and take it , he carry the Israelites thence into Assyria , and the king of Assyria ( who be as Ezra say esur-haddon the son of Senacherib , Ezra 4.2 . who he also call verse 10. the great and noble ashapper ) send thither colony to inhabit that region from Babylon , and from Cuthah , and from Ava , and from Hemath , and from sepharvaim , 2 king . 17.24 . Ezr. 4.9 , 10. Tremel . annot in 2 king . 17. Cuthah and Ava be part of the desert of Arabia : Hemath and sepharvaim of Syria and Mesopotamia . these heathens as the text show serve not the lord , and therefore the lord send lion among they which slay they , 2 king 17.25 . hereupon they send to the king of Assyria , who send unto they one of the priest of Israel , who be bring thence to dwell among they , and teach they the manner of the god of the land , who do accord , and dwell at Bethel , and teach they to worship the true God , ver . 26.27 , 28. and hence it be that in many point of religion , they agree with god own people , acknowledge the five book of Moses ; use circumcision , and the sacrifice of the law , ver . 32. and look for the Messiah that be to come , John 4.25 . but yet they corrupt those point of religion they have learn , with gross superstition , and idolatry , verse 29. in which respect though the holy ghost say of they , ver . 33. they fear God , because of some part of his worship , which they retain , yet in the next verse it be say that they do not fear God ; because they worship he not accord to his word . this their superstition be the chief cause why the Jew hate they and contemn they : nor can they object worse in their venomous slander than this , thou be a Samaritane , John 8.42 . for such they conclude have the devil , they worship the devil under their idol . now in the history , one of these samaritane it be ; one of that sect and nation that show mercy and compassion to this poor distress man . observe then first , how God beat down the pride of man by such mean and person as we most contemn , and esteem most base of . 1 Cor. 1.27 , 28. see this in sundry instance , 1 Sam 17 9 , 42 , 44 , 49 , 51. the first part or verse , the first part . a assurance of God protection . be a nonlatinalphabet , or a universal proposition , in which be contain a comfortable and excellent promise make by the holy ghost , of security , viz. that god help shall never be want to those who true put their hope and trust in he : he that dwell in tho secret place of the most high , shall abide or lodge under the shadow of the almighty . 1. to the godly , and he that put his trust in God . he ; be he who he will , rich or poor , king or people , God be no respecter of person . 2. that dwell ; for that he must be sure to do so constant , daily , firm , rest and acquiesce in God , persevere in the faith of his promise , and carry that about he , as the snail do his shell , for else he can be assure by this promise . 3. in the secret place ; for his aid and defence be not as some strong hold or castle , which be visible ; it|be a secret and invisible fortress , know only to a faithful soul ; in that he may repose his hope , as a mean and secondary defence ; but he dwell , rely , rest in that help of God , which be secret , and be not see , except to the eye of faith . 4. who can , of the most high ; and upon this he rely , because he be the most high ; above he be , and see all , nothing be hide from he ; and again , above he be , sit in the high throne , and rule all ; all thing be under his foot , he can therefore deliver he from all trouble and danger . yea , and will defend he . and he will do it ; for this faithful man , he that rely and trust in he , shall never be frustrate of his hope , protect he shall be , he shall be safe . 1. he dwell , therefore he shall abide , he shall lodge quiet , secure ; pernoctabit , Ithlonan . 2. he dwell in the secret place , therefore he shall abide under the shadow , in refrigerio , in the cool , the favour , the cover from the heat . 3. he dwell in the secret place of the most high , therefore he shall abide under the shadow of the almighty , i . e . of the all-powerful God , of the god of heaven ; of that God , who name be Shaddai , all-sufficient ; by which name , he make his promise to Abraham , Gen. 17.1 . this proposition be most certain true , the second part . This the prophet prove by three witness . in the next place the psalmist explain it , and that no man doubt of it , descend to prove it by three witness : first , of a just man ; second , of the prophet ; three , of God himself . 1. first , he bring in the just man thus speak in his own person , i will say unto the lord , he be my refuge , my fortress , my God , in he will i trust : be it so ? shall he that dwell in the secret of the most high , of the just man , that apply the protection to himself . abide under the shadow of the almighty ? therefore i will say in the person of all just man , to the lord that have no superior , that have no peer ; to that lord , to who command all thing be subject , and who can be command by none ; i will say to he : 1. thou be my refuge : if pursue , i will fly to thou , as a sanctuary . 2. thou be my fortress : if set upon , i will take my self to thou , as a strong tower . 3. thou be my God : if assault by man or devil , thou the most high , thou almighty , a god able to defend i , and therefore i will hope in thou ; i will dwell , trust , rely upon thou , and this thy promise , in every temptation and danger . 2. next to assert the truth of this , by the attestation of the prophet , who enumerate the particular from which he shall be deliver . he bring in the attestation of the prophet for be move by the holy ghost , he say as much , sure he shall deliver thou ; and then fall upon the particular , from which the godly man shall be deliver , set down in many metaphor . 1. he shall deliver thou from the snare of the fowler ; from the deceit of evil man or devil . 2. from the noisome pestilence ; all danger to which we be incident , by plague , war , famine . again , when thou be little in thy own eye , as it be , but a chicken : 1. he shall cover thou , as the hen do her young bird , with his feather , and under his wing shall thou trust ; secure from the kite , the rain , the storm , and heat of the sun 2. when thou be grow up , and able to encounter a enemy in the field , he shall help thou to a shield and a buckler , and that shall be his truth , his veracity , thy faith in it ; and which be yet more , thou shall not be afraid . 1. for the terror by night ; any hide , secret tentation , danger , treachery , detraction , conspiracy . 2. nor for the arrow that fly by day ; any open persecution , calamity , proud assault , invasion . 3. nor for the pestilence that walk in darkness ; the machination of wicked man hatch in the dark . 4. nor for the destruction that walk at noonday ; the bold threat and decree of tyrant and persecutor . Moller observe right , that the promise of deliverance here make , do not belong to one or other kind of evil , but to all kind of calamity , open or secret , and so may be appliable to any ; some of which steal upon we , as in the night secret ; other overwhelm we , as in the day , open : but the promise be general , as Bellarmine well observe , whether the danger come by day or night , those that trust in God be arm with his shield of truth against it . for if God be with we , who can be against we ? Rom. 8. the prophet go on , and confirm the godly in their security , by the dissimilitude , or unlike condition of wicked man ; when thou shall be safe , they shall fall . but with the wicked it be not so , they shall fall . 1. a thousand shall fall at thy side , on thy left hand , overcome by adversity . 2. ten thousand on thy right hand , flatter into sin by prosperity : but not the night fear , nor the arrow by day , shall not come nigh thou . 3. and which be another cause of comfort and pleasure ; Xvii Arg. of Chr. deity . last here we have the seventeenth argument of the deity of Christ : in that he threaten to punish these wicked deceiver : for none but God only be able to do it , and therefore it manifest his divine omnipotency . that which the heretic object concern Moses strike the egyptian with plague , be of no weight : neither of Peter his slay of Ananias : nor Pauls strike Elymas with blindness . for we know , that the prophet and apostle wrought miracle , not of themselves , but by the power of God . whereas Christ threaten to do this by his own power , Io. 5.19 . mat 10.1 and 16.17 . for whatsoever thing the father do , these also do the son likewise . yea the son give power unto other to do the like thing . and all the church shall know ] the end and use of God vengeance on sinner , be to declare both his omniscience of the hide thing of the hart : as also his omnipotency and justice in render to every one accord to his work : even as God say to Pharaoh : Exo. 9.16 . Iii . it be not interest alone that obstruct the happy ʋnion that ought to be among Christians ; and occasion those division among they ; but there be also a necessity , that man shall divest themselves of their prejudice , and prepossession of mind : for these do always keep those difference alive ; and be the constant food and nourishment which do not only support , but increase and augment they ; till they grow at last beyond all hope and possibility of composure . will all person endeavour hearty to take the beam out of their own eye , they will see clear to remove the moat out of their brother ; and will hereby advance a large step towards reconciliation , by come to a good understanding , and a clear discovery of what it be they differ about : controversy be many time in mere word rather than in substance , and be rather the effect of prejudice and misunderstanding one another meaning than in the thing themselves about which we seem to disagree : for prejudice be a ill opinion of person or thing , arise from a false judgement which we entertain concern they ; which make they appear to we of monstrous and deform shape and colour ; it be a certain jaundice of the mind , which stain every thing with the disagreable appearance with which , not the thing themselves , but the conception we have of they be vitiate ; and our judgement by that misinformation deceive and abuse : for when the mind come to be infect with prejudice , it render those thing ill and unlawful , which to a mind clear from those obstruction and gross humour of the ʋnderstand , appear as in reality they be in themselves , innocent , good , lawful ; and many time expedient and necessary ; or however at the worst , in their own nature simple indifferent . this be the common lot of all those thing in divine worship , which be not of the essence , but circumstantial of religion ; which be the thing that raise the great dissension and difference among Christians . it be at once the occasion of wonder and of pity , to see Christians who all agree in the main , Eph. 4.5 . that there be one lord , one faith , one baptism , one God the father , of who be all thing , 1 Cor. 8.6 . and we in he : and one lord Jesus Christ , by who be all thing , and we by he ; who all consent in one common end , and design , which be eternal happiness in heaven ; and one hope of this common salvation ; that yet they shall disagree , and fall out by ( and about ) the way that lead to those bless region of light and immortality . when Joseph ( the type of our bless Jesus ) have give his brethren change of raiment , and provision for their journey , in order to bring they out of the land of famine , to the country of Goshen , it be not a unnecessary command which he lay upon they , Gen. 45.24 . see that you fall not out by the way . shall they now have fall out into dispute and difference about their apparel , because it be not all of a size , color , or fashion ; or whether they shall travel the same way back again , and not seek another ? will not the one have be as ridiculous a dispute , as the other dangerous ? and yet this very folly there be too many guilty of ; who leave the old road of charity , because their brethren will not comply with they in their novel dress of religion , seek out a new road to heaven , which the foot of faint or martyr never tread before . thus shall you see some people start at the very word ceremony , as if it will convey the plague into their ear , and damnation into their soul ; and yet they themselves can perform no religious office of bodily worship without it ; and if saint Paul be to be credit , God expect we shall offer our body a live sacrifice , Rom. 12.3 . holy , and acceptable unto God , which be our reasonable service . a ceremony be nothing more than a solemn way or manner of perform religious duty , which word some derive â carendo , quia religio non potest exerceri caeremonijs carens , because no religion can be perform without some ceremony ; though other it may be more apt â charitate , from that charity which the agreement of people in one way and manner of worship , do natural produce among they : or it may be from the old latin word Caerus , which be the same with sanctus , holy , for which reason the Latins call their holiday , caeremoniosi die , and it be no more but a rite or custom appropriate to holy ʋse . some there be , who to render it odious , derive it from the heathen goddess , Ceres ; how like scholar , or rather schoolboy , let the learned judge . but suppose it be true , be it a good argument , the heathens have ceremony , therefore the Christians must have none ? by the same reason , the heathens worship false god , therefore Christians must not worship the true , for all worship be ceremony . but when once this prejudice be fix to the word , it be no more but fix the word to what people dislike , and it present look black and ugly ; and become a Mormo , a bugbear to their thought ; and like child , they start and cry out at the shadow which they make themselves : but if they can add the word popish , that it be a popish ceremony , then it be impious , ʋnlawful , superstitious , and damnable , and all that can be say to render it execrable , and abominable : when as in truth all that papist do , be not popery , nor abominable , and lawful ; but however , by the strength of this prejudice , those thing which be true primitive , be make popish ; kneel at the receive the holy eucharist , bow at the name of Jesus , and the whole book of the liturgy , though for the generality express word of scripture , be make ceremony , and popish ; and then , how innocent soever , if a tumult can but be make , away with they , crucify they : whereas sober people ought to consider that there be a necessity of some solemn way , rite , Modes , and method of express our devotion , and worship ; and since these Modes , or way , be ceremony , and some ceremony be not only lawful but necessary , why not these ? which have be approve in all age of the church , ever since the time of apostolical purity , rather than any new one of private invention , in opposition to public authority , which think fit to continue , establish , and command these to be use in the church ? i be not ignorant that here be a strong suspicion of malice , as well as mistake ; and it be much to be doubt , that there be a combination of interest , as well as prejudice , in some of the principal fomenter of division ; who appear so transport against all ceremony ; and that though they know better thing , yet they mighty endeavour to maintain these prejudice in the mind of the easy multitude ; who hang their faith upon the oracle of their lip ; and in this be absolute papist , believe with a implicit faith the infallible decree of the head of their church : for upon this depend the benevolence which their follower do so free bestow upon they for this industrious flattery , that these prejudice be mark and sign of their Christian liberty , and be escape from the yoke of antichristian tyranny , and egyptian darkness , into the glorious light of gospel truth ; as they be please to call even these prejudice and mistake ; though to the certain loss of christian charity , and ʋnity ; the great hazard of temporal peace , and the ruin of religion it self , as i shall hereafter more full make appear . this dangerous prejudice , which do infect the mind of man , as it have its foundation in misunderstand of thing ; so it receive strength from two thing which be almost as difficult to change and alter , as the course of nature : and they be , education and custom . of the last the holy ghost speak most plain by the prophet Jeremy . Jer. 13.23 . can the ethiopian change his skin , or the leopard his spot ? then may you do good , who be accustom , ( or as the hebrew word signify , teach ) to do evil , learn to do well . of the other , that of the heathen poet may pass for good divinity , have be confirm to be truth , by the iversal testimony of all age : quô semèl est imbuta recens , servabit odorem Testa diu . — the first season principle and prejudice which we receive in our youth , stick close to we for a long time after . the wise philosopher tell we , the soul of man be Rasa Tabula , like a white sheet of paper , out of which therefore it must be more than common art , that can so clear take out the first writing , as to superinduce a new copy fair and legible . this be the true reason why any person find it so difficult to quit those notion of religion , which have be establish in his mind from his early infancy : there be a marvellous agreement , and natural kindness to those opinion which we suck in with our milk ; they be like foster brother , to who it have be observe , there be as strong a inclination as to the natural ; we play and converse with they from our cradle , and as soon as we can go alone , we take they by the hand ; we sleep with they in our bosom , and contract a insensible friendship with they , a please familiarity , which take off all deformity ; we love they , and we like they , and their very blackness be a beauty , as it be with the african nation , to who even that which we judge deformity , appear more lovely than the most delicate european beauty . what can be more impertinent and ridiculous than the Koran of that great impostor , mahume ? and yet a natural Turk , bear to those prejudice of education , and custom , believe he the great of the prophet ; and the only friend of God : and though he have a great veneration for Christ , yet will he spit in your face , and call you hog , ( the most disgraceful term they think they can affront a christian with ) if you shall contradict he , or go about to convince he of his error , by show the absurdity and impossibility of what he call his religion . what can be more barbarous and savage , than the rite of the pagan idolater ? and yet , you may almost as soon remove a mountain , as one of they , from what have be the religion of his ancestor ; and he , ever since he know any . what can be more unreasonable , than some of the tenet of the Romish church ? more ridiculous and antic than some part of their great solemnity ; more fabulous extravagant than some of their legend , and most of their miracle ? and yet we see , how tenacious not only most of the vulgar , but even of the learned among they be ; that they will not retrench even the foppery of their faith , as they call it , to advance one step towards a ʋnion with the whole catholique church , of the past , and present age ; but on the contrary , by the anathema be of the convention of Trent , have cut off all hope and possibility of such a accord ; by establish the very Minutiae and Quisquiliae , the punctilio and pare of their religion , as thing necessary to be believe in order to salvation . what can be more hard and cruel , than for those who profess to be of the reform religion , to run to the very extremity of ruin themselves , by mutual division ? and so far to weaken themselves and disparage their religion by intestine quarrel , to open a easy way for conquest to the enemy both of our religion and country ; for some difference in circumstance of divine worship ? and yet we see , such be the strength of some man prejudice , and the education of other , in the late unhappy time of our misery and distraction , ( from which we be rescue by so many miracle ) that they will rather venture upon the same tempestuous sea again , from whence we have so late escape shipwreck ; nay use all art and argument to push man headlong from the shore , into the foam main again , which they have but just recover , still wet and shiver , rather than by a compliance with the modest command of the wise pilot , their lawful superior , even in the most indifferent thing , endeavour to repair the leaky vessel of the church and state ; by advance their peace and happiness by a bless ʋnion . the glory of God , and the salvation of man soul , which certain be never to be promote by variance , hatred , emulation , sedition , swell , and tumult , be of no force or power , in comparison of their prejudice ; but they will in obedience to the tyranny of their education and custom , persist in these practice , fear neither the law of God or man ; the transgression of which they call zeal , and their disobedience religion ; but by their example teach other to do the same , and confirm they in it . nor be custom a less tyrant over man mind , to the introduce of prejudice , than education : for hereby man acquire habit , which be a kind of second nature : and by long continuance be so rivet into our good esteem , that they be not to be put off without great difficulty , how inconsiderable soever they be ; this be not more visible in any thing , than the use and opinion which several nation have of their several habit of apparel ; for to a mind unprepossed with custom , all habit which be decent , convenient and useful , appear equal and indifferent ; yet do a Spaniard abhor the fashion of the french , nor do the other use to be behind hand in his requital of the same nature : a Turk believe a turban and a long vest the most ornamental dress in the world , and wish it as a great curse to his enemy to have the plague of a christian hat : and so it be with other nation ; the indian prefer his adressful nakedness before the cumbersome vestment of the europaean , the stone in his lip , nose , and ear , he esteem better set , than all the artist in the world can do in gold . all these be pure the effect of a prejudice custom , which make people gaze even to rudeness and incivility upon foreigner when they appear among we ; and with a ridiculous curiosity , spy imaginary defect and deformity in the unaccustomed dress of other country : nay , even the antiquate fashion of our own , though once the high and only become mode in their time ; yet by custom become so uncouth , as to help we to proverb of derision , by way of comparison . i choose to instance the rather in this strength of prejudice acquire by custom , because it bear a analogy to the prejudice which possess people mind about the habit and outward dress of religion . thus he who have not be use to see a churchman in his canonical habit , look upon he as a strange undress monster ; he who have not be acquaint with the surplice , and other decent vestment of the church , use in the celebration of divine service , especial if he have by his education be principle with a dislike and detestation of they , look upon they as antic and useless formality : he who have be accustom to sit at the lord be table , and to hand the bread and wine to his next neighbour , think kneel a unnecessary part of his devotion ; and if he have be teach so , superstition and idolatry ; and to receive it from the hand of the minister , with the usual benediction , a tedious and troublesome custom : he who have be long use to sudden and extempore prayer , and believe they a fruit of the spirit , and have it may be acquire a talon that way , and please himself and other with it ; look upon be tie up to the word of the liturgy as a hard confinement , and the imprisonment of the spirit , because it tie up his tongue and his part from their accustom liberty of say any thing to God almighty , and offer to he the sacrifice of fool : which make he abhor the sacrifice of praise and thanksgiving when tie by the authority of the church , and the cord of royal command , Psal. 18.27 . to the horn of the altar . he who have be accustom to certain study , passionate , and languish tone , his ear be grate with the music of the church ; though ancient use in the jewish church in the worship of God ; the employment of angel , and commend to we by the holy ghost , by the mouth of the psalmist , upon so many instrument : conclude the psalm which will reach they both as to the matter , manner , psalm 150.6 . and time , let every thing that have breath praise the lord , praise you the lord . and for this reason it be , that the politic head of division and separation , both papist and other dissenter , do industrious prohibit or estrange their people , from join with we in our worship ; well know , that the prejudice of custom will in a little time do more to persuade weak mind to a dislike , than all the reason in the world can to approve ; and when once people have go so far in custom , to forsake the congregation and public service and worship of God , for their own or the private fancy of their teacher , then do they labour all they can to justify their separation against those who either oppose they , or endeavour to reclaim they ; if they meet with weak opponent , they do not only fortify themselves in their prejudice , but stagger the other with their own weak defence ; and so talk themselves into a custom of disobedience : if they meet with such as be too hard for they at dint of argument , that be carnal reason , and worldly wisdom ; and the defect of reason to make good their pretence , they either supply with wander and shift wrangle , or however with the strength of obstinacy : as i have hear one , who be too close hunt from his shift and evasion , plain say , it be a folly to talk to he of reason , for he will hear no reason , for he be sure he be in the right : which though some may look upon as a subject fit for laughter , for my own part , i look upon they as the great object of compashon ; their condition be certain most deplorable , who be past the use of their reason ; and one may have but too just ground to apprehend , that more of the separation be in the same condition of obstinacy contract by long custom and education . and we have a great deal of reason both to pray for they , and with the apostle , to be deliver from ʋnreasonable man . so that the great influence of prejudice , whether by long custom , or education , be most apparent ; and till such time as man can be persuade to abandon these fortification and barricado of their soul , they will remain impregnable against all the attempt of truth and reason , which may be make use of , to endeavour to bring they to a christian compliance , into reconciliation , peace , and ʋnity .

CHAP. IV. where interest combine with prejudice have take deep root , they be very hard to be overcome , or extirpate from the mind of man ; but there be two thing yet behind , which be more stubborn , because more natural to we ; as be usual interweave with the very temper of our nature , the vice of our complexion , as well as contract with time . for no man be bear with any design in his head , nor be prejudice and custom , éx traduce , derive from our parent : but pride and ambition be vice to which the very constitution of some man body do more powerful incline they ; some nature be infect with hot and tumultuary spirit , a restless and a boil blood , impatient of the rein of government , as the poet long ago observe in his character of youth , Caereus invitium flecti , monitoribus asper : and it be descernable many time in the early sally of life , and so soon as reason begin to show it self , pride and ambition begin to appear and discover themselves ; thus you shall see some nature meek and malleable , soft and tender ; other , as if with Romulus they have suck the wolf , will like he , play the king among their companion ; and at that age , be so ambitious of the little honour , and superiority above their equal in age , and sometime their superior in quality , that they will venture the severity of the Ferula , and their blood too , in the quarrel , to be Caesar aut nullus : and there be scarce a public school , where one may not be furnish with a speciman of this nature . now to subdue these congenial inclination , do doubtless require not only the great force of virtue , but the great stock of grace : and many time without the last , all the power of the first prove too weak a dam to stop these impetuous torrent of natural ambition , and pride , when swell with the tide of invite and feazible temptation ; and violent raise above their shore , with the turbulent air of popular breath ; the most dangerous tempest that can blow upon these swell and foam wave . i join these two together , because they be seldom find asunder ; and it be not common to find a proud man that be not ambitious , or a ambitious person that be not proud ; and one can rare be tempt by one , without the assistance of the other , and if they be not twin , i can decide their pretension which shall be the elder brother ; but which soever be the first bear of folly , pride usual set ambition upon action : and what the one project the other execute . for that any person shall despise the precept of virtue and religion , the principle of loyalty and obedience ; per fas and nefas , ( the common ladder of ambition ) aspire at high thing , great place of trust , honour , dignity , or command in the world ; must certain be the effect of pride : for such temper be always opiniatre of their own merit ; haughty mind think and believe therefore , that they deserve whatsoever they can desire , and more than they have ; and the high they rise , still the more proud and ambitious they grow ; look upon their past acquisition , as the just reward of their wisdom and conduct ; which still swell they with big desire , and large thought ; so that in reality ambition know no Hercules pillar , because pride can write a nè plùs ultrà upon their merit : for this be the dropsy of the mind , and the more man drink , than the more they thirst : — totum licet hauserit Hermum , ardebit majore siti . — thus the young macedonian swell with his victory over the fortunate persian and his ally , and be tell of more world , aestuat infelix angusto limit mundi . his ambition boil over at his eye , that he be confine to one : nay , so strange be he transport with this unruly tumour , that nothing will satisfy he but to be a God , as if he have intention to depose his adopt father , and conquer heaven too , when he come there . now it be the constant temper of these hot complexion , to make every thing stoop to their design , because they think every thing ought to bow to their merit ; nor will they spare even religion it self , to which they will yet seem bend , only to bend it to their interest ; and they do most frequent abuse that sacred name , because by that method they be enable more easy to abuse other with their well act hypocrisy , and double-gilt dissimulation : and it be the sole prerogative of heaven to know man heart , their great masterpiece be to cover their ambition with the soft mantle of religion , and to act that part of their live so to the life , as rather to outdo the most sincere and innocent , as to what be visible , than by any remissness in their outward appearance , to hazard a discovery of what lodge within : nor indeed be it possible to distinguish such golden sinner from the most splendid saint , without a narrow consideration whither their design must lead they , and in what they will end ; and this be the true reason why the innocent and wellmeaning among the ordinary rank of people be so often impose upon , surprise with , and carry on in a good opinion of their proud and ambitious leader , who cover those horrid vice , with the glorious name of zeal for the lord of host , fervency of spirit , and active piety for the cause of God : for if they will consider , whither of necessity these principle of disobedience , which they infuse into they , both against their king and their church , will at the last lead they ; and of what nature the practise be , which be deduce from they ; it be impossible that people shall be seduce by such troop to adore and almost deify those person , who main design be only to serve their own present interest , and the future aim of their ambitious pride . but lest any person shall think the expression of deify too harsh , i can bring evidence from those who hear it from the person mouth that speak it , who affirm , that be do believe , that if our saviour be upon earth , he can not speak more heavenly than such a one ( name the person ) who be then the admire orator of the place ; CHAP. x. have proceed thus far in show the design of the outward government of the church , to have a political respect , as well as temporal law , to the happiness and well-being of humane society , as well as a religious respect to future Felicity , and that therefore upon this double account our obedience be due to the ecclesiastîcal law as well as the temporal ; to which yet no dissenter will own disobedience to be lawful , because without law , no live ; and for the same reason therefore , obedience to both , be a necessary part of religion ; in the next place let we see how necessary therefore this ʋnity in point of government in the church be upon this political account , in order to the happiness of the society , which be the end of all law and all religion in this present state of life . and this , i conceive , can better be do then by show the dangerous influence which Disunion in point of government in the church , have upon the civil affair of any nation . as true religion make man the best Subjects , steady in their loyalty , and allegiance , faithful in their trust and service , industrious to promote the common good , obedient to their superior , kind to their equal , and humble to their inferior , and always religious observer of the law of God and man : so false opinion , when entertain for true religion , ever render man the worst . and if there be nothing more in it , yet will it be the great interest of prince to endeavour to propagate christian religion in its great purity ; and to discountenance error , heresy , sect , and schism , as the best expedient to secure themselves , and establish their throne upon the immovable basis and sure foundation of the good conscience of their subject : for this surpass all the obligation of law , all the power of arm , and all the art of policy .
